subject: skills/leash_for_hooks/collectors/hook_config.py — module cumulative cyclomatic exceeds D-003 budget
principal: After D-003 flipped the audit budget from LOC to McCabe cyclomatic complexity (≤10 per function, ≤30 cumulative per module), two violators surfaced. The per-function violations have been refactored (commit d9b55a7): collectors/hook_config.py:_walk_settings split into _walk_settings (CC=6) + _walk_event_matchers (CC=4) + _walk_inner_hooks (CC=4); resolvers/data_point.py:resolve split into _validate_target (CC=5) + _scan_jsonl (CC=5) + resolve (CC=5). All functions are now ≤10. However hook_config.py module-cumulative cyclomatic complexity remains 34 (vs D-003's ≤30 budget): the module performs intrinsically branchy work — INPUT-pattern discovery (4), source-state hashing (3), 3-level type-guarded JSON walking (14 across 3 helpers), and per-row provenance verification (5) — and per-function splits add base-1 counts faster than they reduce branch counts. Further refactor without changing behavior or removing defensive isinstance guards on external JSON input is not visible.
interest: skills/leash_for_hooks/verify.py reports 1 fail / 19 self-checks (down from 2 fail before commit d9b55a7). The single remaining failure is module-cumulative, not per-function. Any SDLC pipeline gate that treats verify.py exit code as green/red signal remains red. The gap is 4 cumulative points (34 vs 30); the module would need 12-13% structural reduction to clear. Pylint, NIST SP 500-235, and McCabe 1976 all name per-function CC thresholds; module-cumulative is operator-authored discretion in D-003's payoff, not a vendor-anchored standard.
payoff: Two paths. (a) Domain-aware budget: open a new debt to relax DEFAULT_COLLECTOR_BUDGET (raise to e.g. 40 cumulative) on the basis that collector modules with external-input type-guarding intrinsically exceed 30; document the relaxation in audit.py with the citation gap acknowledged. (b) Structural compression: simplify hook_config.py beyond branch-shifting — e.g., centralize the type-guard checks into a single validation pass that produces a normalized dict of (event, matcher_list) tuples, then iterate the normalized form without per-level isinstance branches. (a) is cheaper and acknowledges the standards gap honestly; (b) is more work and doesn't generalize. Recommendation: (a).
